questions.question: Explain the need for friend functions in operator overloading with an example.
keyPoints: Friend functions allow access to private members
They provide operand symmetry (a+b = b+a)
Example: when left operand is not class object (5+obj)

questions.question: Write a C++ program to overload + operator using friend function for a complex number class.
keyPoints: Define Complex class with real and imag members
Declare friend function prototype inside class
Define function outside class returning Complex by reference
Show usage in main with chaining

questions.question: Why should operator<< and operator>> be overloaded as friend functions?
keyPoints: Cannot use member function for cout << obj
Symmetry with stream objects
Need to return ostream/istream reference for chaining
Allows both 'cout << obj' and 'obj << cout' behavior

questions.question: What are the restrictions on operator overloading in C++?
keyPoints: Cannot create new operators
At least one operand must be user-defined type
Cannot change precedence or associativity
Cannot overload ?: , :: , . , .* , sizeof

questions.question: Explain the difference between overloading binary operators using member function vs friend function.
keyPoints: Member function uses implicit 'this', friend has two explicit params
Member: obj + value works but value + obj doesn't
Friend: both directions work due to symmetry
Friend needed when left operand is not class object

questions.question: Write a C++ program to overload insertion (<<) and extraction (>>) operators for a class.
keyPoints: Friend declaration: friend ostream& operator<<(ostream&, const Class&)
Return ostream& for chaining
Friend declaration for >> with istream&
Define functions to read/write member variables

questions.question: Why is it recommended to return by reference in operator overloading?
keyPoints: Enables chained operations (a+b+c)
Avoids unnecessary copying of objects
Improves efficiency and performance
Allows results to be used as lvalue

questions.question: Design a class String with member variables for storing string data. Overload operators + (concatenation), == (equality), and << (output) using friend functions. Write complete C++ program demonstrating all operations.
keyPoints: Class design with char* or string member
Constructor and destructor
Friend operator+ for concatenation returning String by reference
Friend operator== returning bool
Friend operator<< for output
Main showing all operations with chaining

questions.question: Explain operator overloading using friend functions in C++. Write a complete program to overload arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /) for a class representing a 2D Point with x and y coordinates.
keyPoints: Explain friend function concept and syntax
Explain why friend preferred for symmetry
Point class with x, y members
Overload +, -, *, / as friend functions
Return by reference for chaining
Demonstrate operations in main

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program to overload stream operators (<< and >>) for a Student class with name, roll number, and marks. Use friend function concept and demonstrate input/output operations.
keyPoints: Define Student class with required members
Overload << as friend: ostream& operator<<(ostream&, const Student&)
Overload >> as friend: istream& operator>>(istream&, Student&)
Return stream references for chaining
Handle proper input/output of all members
Main demonstrating cout and cin operations

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of using friend functions for operator overloading. When should one prefer member functions over friend functions?
keyPoints: Advantages: operand symmetry, access to private members, can overload << >>
Disadvantages: breaks encapsulation slightly, less intuitive
Prefer member when: operator should be part of class behavior, not symmetric
Prefer friend when: need symmetry, left operand not class, stream operators
